home.headline: Designing Thinkers,
home.subline: I am a User Experience Designer making a deliberate transition into IB MYP Design education — bringing structured design thinking, real-world problem-solving, and a deep commitment to inquiry-based learning into the classroom.

pillars.title: Design Thinking
pillars.desc: Systematic problem-solving methodologies tailored for student cognitive development.

pillars.title: Inquiry-Based Learning
pillars.desc: Fostering curiosity through open-ended investigations and critical questioning.

pillars.title: User-Centered Approach
pillars.desc: Applying empathy to solve real human needs through iterative design cycles.

pillars.title: Mentorship
pillars.desc: Guiding the next generation of thinkers with industry-standard design practice.

lessonHighlights.title: Rapid Prototyping Sprint
lessonHighlights.desc: A high-energy lesson focused on building low-fidelity physical models within strict time constraints.

lessonHighlights.title: Empathy Interview Lab
lessonHighlights.desc: Methodologies for gathering deep user insights through structured qualitative research techniques.

lessonHighlights.title: UX Critique Circle
lessonHighlights.desc: Facilitating constructive feedback sessions using specific rubric-based assessment criteria.

about.headline:
A deliberate transition,
not a career pivot.
about.intro: I am a User Experience Designer currently working on complex B2B systems at Ideaforge Technology — where my work involves structured problem-solving, understanding user needs, and iterative solution development.
paragraphs: Over time, I realised that what excites me most is not just building solutions, but shaping how people think while solving problems. Through mentoring interns at work — guiding them from problem identification to refined solutions — I saw how powerful it is to develop someone's thinking process rather than hand them answers.

That realisation led me to IB Design education. The MYP framework uniquely positions design not as an aesthetic skill but as a way of thinking — emphasising inquiry, design cycles, and reflection. This closely mirrors real-world design processes, and I see it as the right environment to help students become thoughtful problem-solvers.

This transition is a conscious shift, not a temporary move. I have treated the transition itself as a structured design problem: understanding how students learn, how design thinking can be taught effectively, and how classroom experiences can be intentionally designed.

education.institution: IDC School of Design, IIT Bombay
education.detail: Design as a structured thinking discipline. Strong emphasis on research, human needs, systems thinking, and ethics. Mentored by faculty contributing to India's National Education Policy (NEP 2020).

points: Translated complex, ambiguous requirements into structured, user-centred problem statements — the same process the IB MYP Design cycle asks students to practise.
Led design work on high-stakes government use cases, reframing abstract requirements into clear design challenges.
Mentored UX/UI interns from IDC and NID, guiding them through design thinking processes using questioning-based feedback rather than prescriptive answers.
Applied iterative design: research → ideation → prototyping → evaluation — exactly the MYP Design cycle.

ibReadiness: Clear understanding of IB Programme structure (PYP, MYP, DP)
Primary focus on MYP Design as a foundational thinking discipline
Familiar with MYP Design Criteria A–D
Familiar with Approaches to Learning (ATL) skills
Familiar with Global Contexts and conceptual learning
Actively preparing for IB Professional Development certification

philosophy.headline: Design education is not about producing designers.
philosophy.subline: It is about developing students who can observe carefully, empathise responsibly, define problems clearly, and reflect before acting.
philosophy.quote: Whether students become doctors, engineers, scientists, civil servants, or policymakers — the habits they develop through design thinking stay deeply relevant. Design gives them a transferable mindset, not a vocational skill.

pillars.title: Critical Thinking
pillars.desc: I would guide students to slow down assumptions, question what they think they already know, and understand context before attempting any design response.

pillars.title: Empathy First
pillars.desc: I would build activities that ask students to observe real human needs before designing anything — considering diverse perspectives and questioning their own assumptions first.

pillars.title: Process Over Product
pillars.desc: Valuing the documentation of failure, iteration, and reflection as much as the final outcome. A strong process leads to meaningful products — not the other way around.

pillars.title: Student Agency
pillars.desc: I would anchor problems in students' real lives so design becomes a tool, not a subject. I believe that when students connect with the problem, engagement shifts from 'I have to' to 'I want to solve this.'

philosophy.closingStatement: In industry, success is measured by the output — what gets built. But the real value lies in the thinking behind those outputs. IB Design would give me a structured environment to cultivate that thinking at the right age — when students are forming their problem-solving mindset.

units.intro: A progression of inquiry-driven design units across MYP levels, guiding students from structured observation to independent problem-solving. Units are designed to progressively build students' ability to move from structured inquiry to independent thinking, aligned with the IB MYP Design cycle and assessment criteria.

units.title: The Anatomy of Everyday Objects
units.desc: Students deconstruct household items to understand form, function, and the basics of technical sketching.

units.title: Eco Enclosures
units.desc: Introduction to sustainable materials through the design and construction of small-scale modular packaging.

units.title: Designing for Everyday Accessibility
units.desc: Students explore how everyday objects may not work equally well for all users and learn to design improvements that enhance accessibility and usability — moving from observation to empathy to a real, tested solution.
units.statementOfInquiry: Design solutions reflect how individuals understand and respond to everyday challenges.
units.unitOverview: This unit introduces students to the idea that design is not just about making things — it is about solving real human problems. Students explore how everyday objects may not work equally well for all users, and learn to design improvements that enhance accessibility and usability for elderly users, children, people with temporary injuries, and people with disabilities. Good design is inclusive design.
units.summativeTask: Students identify an accessibility issue in an everyday object, define the problem clearly, generate multiple design ideas, create a prototype, and evaluate its effectiveness against their own design specifications.

inquiryQuestions.factual: What are common accessibility challenges in everyday objects?
inquiryQuestions.debatable: Is a simple design always a better design?

weeks.title: Inquiring & Analysing
weeks.desc: Students are introduced to accessibility as a design lens. They interact with poorly designed objects, audit everyday items, understand different user types, and define a clear problem statement.

lessons.title: Introduction to Accessibility
lessons.goal: Introduce the concept of accessibility in design
lessons.what: Students interact with poorly designed objects or examples, discuss frustration and usability, and begin to see design as something that affects human experience.
lessons.activityDetail: Students interact with a poorly designed object and document: what was difficult to use, and why it failed the user.
lessons.outcome: Students realise that design affects experience — not just aesthetics.

lessons.title: Observing Design Problems
lessons.goal: Identify real-world usability issues
lessons.what: Students examine 2–3 objects in the classroom or at home and identify usability challenges through structured observation.
lessons.activityDetail: For each object: Who is the user? What is the problem? How could it be improved?
lessons.outcome: Students begin thinking like designers — observing before solving.

lessons.title: Understanding Users
lessons.goal: Build empathy for different user types
lessons.what: Students are introduced to different user groups — elderly, children, people with temporary injuries, people with disabilities — and create simple user personas.
lessons.activityDetail: Students define: user type, their specific needs, and their limitations. One persona per student.
lessons.outcome: Shift from 'object thinking' to 'user thinking'.

lessons.title: Defining the Problem
lessons.goal: Move from observation to a clear problem definition
lessons.what: Students convert their observations into structured problem statements using a guided template.
lessons.activityDetail: Template: '[User] needs a way to [need] because [reason].' Students share and refine with a peer.
lessons.outcome: A clear, user-centred problem statement that will guide the rest of the unit.

weeks.title: Developing Ideas
weeks.desc: Students move from problem to possibility. They generate multiple design ideas rapidly, evaluate them logically, and define measurable success criteria for their chosen direction.
weeks.focus: Moving from divergent thinking → evaluated choice → specification

lessons.title: Idea Generation
lessons.goal: Explore multiple solutions without filtering
lessons.what: Students sketch as many ideas as possible in a short time, prioritising quantity over quality to unlock divergent thinking.
lessons.activityDetail: 8 ideas sketched in 8 minutes. No judgement. Speed forces creativity.
lessons.outcome: A wide range of possible solutions — some expected, some surprising.

lessons.title: Selecting the Best Idea
lessons.goal: Evaluate ideas logically and justify a decision
lessons.what: Students compare their ideas against usability and feasibility criteria and choose the most promising direction.
lessons.activityDetail: Rate each idea against: usability for the target user, and feasibility to build. Select and justify the highest-scoring idea.
lessons.outcome: Students practise decision-making with reasoning — not just instinct.

lessons.title: Design Specification
lessons.goal: Define measurable success criteria before building
lessons.what: Students write a list of specific, measurable criteria that their final design must meet — creating their own evaluation framework.
lessons.activityDetail: Students list 4–6 measurable success criteria. Example: 'Can be operated with one hand', 'Suitable for users aged 60+'.
lessons.outcome: A clear specification that will be used in Lesson 11 to evaluate the final design.

weeks.title: Creating & Evaluating
weeks.desc: Students build, test, refine, and present. The focus is on the thinking behind each decision — not the polish of the final product.
weeks.focus: Moving from idea → prototype → tested solution → reflection

lessons.title: Prototype Creation
lessons.goal: Build a first tangible version of the solution
lessons.what: Students create a low-fidelity prototype using simple materials — paper, cardboard, tape, clay. The goal is to make the idea testable, not perfect.
lessons.activityDetail: Build a working model using available materials. Focus on communicating function, not finish.
lessons.outcome: The idea becomes tangible — and testable.

lessons.title: Testing and Feedback
lessons.goal: Understand how real users respond to the design
lessons.what: Students test each other's prototypes, role-playing as the target user. The designer observes silently and takes notes.
lessons.activityDetail: Tester uses the prototype and narrates their experience. Designer does not explain — only observes and documents.
lessons.outcome: Students see design flaws they couldn't see as the creator.

lessons.title: Iteration and Improvement
lessons.goal: Improve the solution based on feedback
lessons.what: Students review their testing notes, identify the most important improvement to make, and refine their prototype.
lessons.activityDetail: Students document: what feedback they received, what they chose to change, and why that change improves the design.
lessons.outcome: A refined solution — and evidence of iterative thinking.

lessons.title: Evaluation
lessons.goal: Reflect critically on the final design
lessons.what: Students compare their final design against the success criteria they wrote in Lesson 7 — and honestly assess what worked and what didn't.
lessons.activityDetail: For each specification criterion: Did the design meet it? What evidence supports this? What would you do differently?
lessons.outcome: Students understand the difference between a successful process and a successful product.

lessons.title: Final Presentation
lessons.goal: Communicate the design process and thinking clearly
lessons.what: Students present their full design journey — problem, user, ideas, prototype, testing, and reflection — to the class.
lessons.activityDetail: Each student presents: the problem they identified, the user they designed for, their chosen solution, what they tested, and what they learned.
lessons.outcome: Clarity, confidence, and the ability to articulate design thinking — not just show a product.

successCriteria: Identify a real-world accessibility problem with clarity and evidence
Demonstrate empathy by designing for a specific user type
Generate multiple design ideas and justify the chosen direction
Create a low-fidelity prototype that communicates function
Test the design with a peer and document honest findings
Evaluate the final design against self-defined success criteria
differentiation: Provide structured templates for problem statements, personas, and evaluation for students who need additional support
Encourage open-ended user research, deeper specification writing, and self-directed iteration for advanced learners
Allow oral explanation as an alternative to written reflection for students with language barriers
units.realWorldConnection: Students explore how design impacts elderly users, children, people with temporary injuries, and people with disabilities — understanding that inclusive design is good design.

units.title: Community Wayfinding
units.desc: Developing signage systems and visual maps for a local school context to improve navigation efficiency.

units.title: Designing for Social Impact
units.desc: Students move beyond individual usability to explore how design can solve community problems, address inequality, and improve everyday systems — shifting the question from 'does this work?' to 'who does this work for, and who is excluded?'
units.statementOfInquiry: Design solutions influence systems by addressing real-world challenges and shaping sustainable outcomes.
units.unitOverview: This unit shifts students from individual usability to broader social impact. Students explore how design can solve community problems, address inequality, and improve everyday systems. The central question is no longer just 'does this work?' but 'who does this work for, and who is excluded?' Students investigate real community issues — waste, navigation, safety, accessibility — and design solutions that consider multiple stakeholders and systemic consequences.
units.summativeTask: Students design a solution addressing a real-world community issue (such as waste management, public navigation, safety, or accessibility). They must investigate context, define a clear problem, develop justified ideas, create a prototype, and evaluate its broader impact.

inquiryQuestions.factual: What are common design problems in communities?
inquiryQuestions.conceptual: How can design influence systems and behaviour?
inquiryQuestions.debatable: Can design truly solve social problems?

weeks.title: Inquiry & System Understanding
weeks.desc: Students are introduced to design as a systems-level activity. They map real community problems, understand how systems work, observe real issues, and identify the multiple stakeholders affected.
weeks.focus: Moving from object thinking → systems thinking → stakeholder awareness

lessons.title: Introduction to Social Impact Design
lessons.goal: Understand design beyond objects — as a tool for social change
lessons.what: Discussion on real-world problems — waste, transport, accessibility. Students see examples of design that changed systems, not just products.
lessons.activityDetail: Students list community issues they observe or experience — waste, accessibility, safety, navigation. Map them on a shared board to find patterns.
lessons.outcome: Students begin to see design as a system-level discipline, not just product creation.

lessons.title: Understanding Systems
lessons.goal: Introduce systems thinking as a design lens
lessons.what: Students learn to identify the components of a system — inputs, processes, outputs — and understand how changing one part affects the whole.
lessons.activityDetail: Students choose a familiar system (school canteen, local bus route, classroom waste) and map its inputs, processes, and outputs. Identify where problems occur.
lessons.outcome: Students understand that complexity is normal — and that design must account for it.

lessons.title: Observing Community Problems
lessons.goal: Identify real-world issues through structured observation
lessons.what: Students conduct a field observation or case study of a community problem — in school or the immediate environment.
lessons.activityDetail: Students document: what happens in the system, who is affected, what the consequences are, and what has already been tried. Use photographs, notes, or sketches.
lessons.outcome: Students identify a specific community problem grounded in real evidence — not assumption.

lessons.title: Understanding Stakeholders
lessons.goal: Move beyond 'the user' to see multiple affected parties
lessons.what: Students map all the people affected by a problem — not just the primary user but secondary users, decision makers, and those indirectly impacted.
lessons.activityDetail: Students identify: primary users (most directly affected), secondary users (indirectly affected), and decision makers (who controls the system). Map relationships between them.
lessons.outcome: Students see that design decisions affect many people — and that inclusion requires deliberate thought.

weeks.title: Defining & Researching
weeks.desc: Students move from observation to structured research. They plan how to gather evidence, collect real data, define a complex problem precisely, and set measurable success criteria.
weeks.focus: Moving from observation → structured research → precise problem definition

lessons.title: Research Methods
lessons.goal: Plan structured research to understand the problem deeply
lessons.what: Students learn the difference between primary research (interviews, surveys, observation) and secondary research (existing data, reports). They plan what they need to find out and how.
lessons.activityDetail: Students create a research plan: what do I need to know? Who can I ask? What already exists? What will I do myself? Plan must include at least one primary and one secondary source.
lessons.outcome: Students approach research as a structured process — not random searching.

lessons.title: Data Collection
lessons.goal: Gather real insights from people and sources
lessons.what: Students conduct their planned research — simulating interviews or surveys within the classroom, and reviewing secondary sources.
lessons.activityDetail: Students conduct peer interviews using 5 prepared questions about their chosen community problem. Record responses. Identify patterns across 3+ responses.
lessons.outcome: Students have real evidence to support their problem definition — not just opinion.

lessons.title: Problem Definition
lessons.goal: Define a complex, multi-stakeholder problem with precision
lessons.what: Students synthesise their research into a refined problem statement that captures the system, the affected people, and the root cause.
lessons.activityDetail: Students write a problem statement that goes beyond the symptom. Structure: '[System/context] fails [stakeholder group] because [root cause], resulting in [consequence].'
lessons.outcome: A precise, evidence-based problem statement that will drive the rest of the unit.

lessons.title: Success Criteria
lessons.goal: Define measurable impact — not just product quality
lessons.what: Students write success criteria that reflect both product function and social impact. They also define constraints — what limits their design.
lessons.activityDetail: Students list 5–7 success criteria and 2–3 constraints. At least two criteria must address social impact beyond function (e.g. 'accessible to users with limited mobility', 'replicable without specialist tools').
lessons.outcome: A specification that measures impact, not just aesthetics or function.

weeks.title: Ideation & Development
weeks.desc: Students generate ideas at a systems level, develop the most promising concept in detail, evaluate options rigorously, and justify their final design direction.
weeks.focus: Moving from divergent ideas → developed concept → justified decision

lessons.title: Idea Generation
lessons.goal: Generate a range of system-level solutions — not just product fixes
lessons.what: Students brainstorm solutions that address the root cause — not just the symptom. Encouraged to think at different scales: individual, community, system.
lessons.activityDetail: Generate at least 6 ideas across different scales. Sketch each with a brief annotation. Avoid fixating on one idea too early.
lessons.outcome: A range of possible approaches — some practical, some ambitious, all evidence-based.

lessons.title: Concept Development
lessons.goal: Develop the most promising idea into a detailed concept
lessons.what: Students select one idea and develop it fully — sketching detail, considering materials, and explaining how it addresses each stakeholder.
lessons.activityDetail: Students produce an annotated design sketch showing: how it works, who it serves, what materials it needs, and how it fits into the existing system.
lessons.outcome: A developed concept ready for evaluation — not just a rough sketch.

lessons.title: Idea Evaluation
lessons.goal: Evaluate all ideas rigorously before committing to one
lessons.what: Students compare their top 3 ideas against their success criteria — including social impact criteria.
lessons.activityDetail: Rate each idea against all success criteria (1–3 scale). Include at least one social impact criterion. The highest-scoring idea becomes the working design.
lessons.outcome: A defensible, criteria-based decision — not a personal preference.

lessons.title: Final Design Selection
lessons.goal: Commit to and justify the chosen design direction
lessons.what: Students write a justification that connects their chosen design to the problem, stakeholders, and success criteria.
lessons.activityDetail: Students write 200–300 words explaining: why this design, how it addresses the root cause, which stakeholders it serves, and what trade-offs were made.
lessons.outcome: Students practise the IB expectation of justification — reasoning, not just describing.

weeks.title: Prototyping & Evaluating
weeks.desc: Students build, test, refine, and present their solution — always returning to the question of impact, not just function.
weeks.focus: Moving from concept → prototype → tested solution → impact evaluation

lessons.title: Prototype Creation
lessons.goal: Build a prototype that communicates the system-level solution
lessons.what: Students build a low-to-mid fidelity prototype. For system solutions this may include diagrams, models, or service blueprints — not always a physical object.
lessons.activityDetail: Build a prototype appropriate to the solution type: physical model, poster system, process diagram, or interactive mock-up. Must show how it functions within the system.
lessons.outcome: A testable representation of the solution — at whatever fidelity fits the problem.

lessons.title: Testing with Users
lessons.goal: Understand whether the solution works for real people in context
lessons.what: Students simulate user testing — peers role-play as the target stakeholder group and provide structured feedback.
lessons.activityDetail: Tester role-plays as a primary stakeholder. Designer observes and notes: what worked, what confused them, what was missing. Debrief using: I noticed / I wondered / What if.
lessons.outcome: Evidence-based insight into how the solution performs — and where it fails.

lessons.title: Iteration
lessons.goal: Improve the solution based on evidence
lessons.what: Students identify the most critical gap revealed by testing and make one meaningful improvement — documenting their reasoning.
lessons.activityDetail: Students document: what feedback they received, which gap they chose to address and why, what change they made, and how it improves the design for the stakeholder.
lessons.outcome: A refined solution with evidence of responsive, evidence-based thinking.

lessons.title: Evaluation & Presentation
lessons.goal: Reflect on impact and communicate the full design journey
lessons.what: Students evaluate their solution against all success criteria — including social impact — and present the full design process to the class.
lessons.activityDetail: Evaluation: for each criterion, did the design meet it? What is the evidence? What would change with more time or resources? Presentation: 5 minutes covering problem, stakeholders, solution, testing, and impact.
lessons.outcome: Students can articulate not just what they made, but what difference it makes — and what they would do differently.

assessment.focus: Community problem research and stakeholder analysis
Idea generation, evaluation, and justified selection
Prototype development appropriate to the solution type
Impact evaluation against social and functional criteria

successCriteria: Identify a real community problem supported by research evidence
Map all relevant stakeholders and explain how the problem affects each
Generate multiple design ideas at different scales and justify a chosen direction
Create a prototype appropriate to the solution type that communicates function within a system
Test the solution with a peer and document honest, specific findings
Evaluate impact against both functional and social success criteria
Reflect on what worked, what failed, and what would change with more time
differentiation: Support: provide pre-structured research templates, stakeholder maps, and problem statement frameworks for students who need scaffolding
Advanced: encourage independent community outreach, multi-stakeholder interviews, and systems diagrams that show second-order effects
Language support: allow visual presentations, annotated diagrams, or recorded explanations as alternatives to written evaluation
units.realWorldConnection: Students explore how systems affect people, why some design solutions fail despite good intentions, and how design decisions create ripple effects across communities.
units.progressionNote: This unit intentionally raises the bar from MYP 2. Students work with less guidance, tackle more complex problems, justify decisions against social impact criteria, and present to the class rather than just a partner.

units.title: Circular Economy Systems
units.desc: Moving beyond product design to design entire lifecycles for consumer products, ensuring zero waste.

units.title: Designing Under Constraints
units.desc: Students confront the reality that design never happens in ideal conditions. Working within material, time, user, and environmental constraints, they learn that good design is not just creative — it is strategic decision-making under pressure.
units.statementOfInquiry: Effective design solutions emerge through managing constraints, optimising resources, and making informed decisions.
units.unitOverview: This unit introduces students to the reality that design does not happen in ideal conditions. Real-world design always involves trade-offs — limited materials, restricted budgets, specific user needs, and environmental considerations. Students learn to see constraints not as obstacles but as the conditions that force better, more innovative thinking. The central insight: constraints shape innovation.
units.summativeTask: Students design a solution to a given challenge under strict constraints (limited materials, budget restriction, specific user needs, or time limitation). They must analyse constraints, generate ideas within limits, justify every decision, prototype efficiently, and evaluate how well the solution addresses the constraints it was given.

inquiryQuestions.factual: What are common constraints in design projects?
inquiryQuestions.conceptual: How do constraints influence design decisions?
inquiryQuestions.debatable: Do constraints limit creativity or enhance it?

weeks.title: Understanding Constraints
weeks.desc: Students are introduced to the concept of constraints as a design reality. They shift their mindset from 'anything is possible' to 'good design works within limits'. They analyse real-world examples, classify constraint types, and identify a constrained problem to work on.
weeks.focus: Shifting mindset: constraints as conditions, not obstacles

lessons.title: Introduction to Constraints in Design
lessons.goal: Shift student mindset — constraints are not obstacles, they are conditions
lessons.what: Discussion on real-world design limitations. Students explore examples where constraints led to better solutions — not worse ones.
lessons.activityDetail: Students list every constraint they can think of that limits design: materials, cost, time, user ability, environment, regulation. Group into categories on a shared board.
lessons.outcome: Students understand that every real design project operates under constraints — and that this is normal, not exceptional.

lessons.title: Types of Constraints
lessons.goal: Identify and categorise the main types of design constraints
lessons.what: Students learn to classify constraints into four categories: material, time, cost, and user — and understand how each type shapes design differently.
lessons.activityDetail: Students are given 20 constraint examples and sort them into the four categories. Then identify which category is hardest to work around and justify why.
lessons.outcome: Students have a working vocabulary for constraint types — they can name and categorise the limits they are working within.

lessons.title: Case Study Analysis
lessons.goal: Learn from real-world examples of constrained design
lessons.what: Students analyse 2 real-world design case studies where constraints shaped the final outcome — e.g. a low-cost prosthetic limb, a flat-pack furniture system, a disposable medical device.
lessons.activityDetail: For each case study: What constraints existed? How did the designer respond? What trade-offs were made? What would have been different without the constraints?
lessons.outcome: Students see constraints as design inputs — not limitations to work around, but conditions to design within.

lessons.title: Problem Exploration
lessons.goal: Identify a constrained design challenge to work on for the unit
lessons.what: Students select or are assigned a design challenge with explicit constraints. They frame the challenge clearly — problem + constraints stated together.
lessons.activityDetail: Students write a challenge statement: 'Design a [solution] for [user] that must work within [constraint 1], [constraint 2], and [constraint 3].' Peer review for clarity.
lessons.outcome: A clear, constrained challenge statement that will drive the rest of the unit.

weeks.title: Defining & Planning
weeks.desc: Students move from understanding constraints in general to mapping the specific constraints of their challenge. They research context, define the problem precisely, map every constraint explicitly, and write success criteria that include constraint-awareness.
weeks.focus: Moving from challenge → researched context → explicit constraint map → specification

lessons.title: Research and Context Analysis
lessons.goal: Understand the environment and users the design must serve
lessons.what: Students research the context of their design challenge — who uses it, where it is used, what already exists, and why existing solutions fall short.
lessons.activityDetail: Students map: the environment of use, the primary user and their specific needs, existing solutions and their shortcomings, and key contextual factors that will affect design decisions.
lessons.outcome: A clear picture of the context — so design decisions are grounded in evidence, not assumption.

lessons.title: Defining the Design Problem
lessons.goal: Write a precise problem statement that includes constraints
lessons.what: Students synthesise their context research into a refined problem statement that names the user, the need, and the specific constraints that make it challenging.
lessons.activityDetail: Structure: '[User] needs [solution] that [addresses need] within [constraint 1] and [constraint 2], because [root reason].' Peer review for precision.
lessons.outcome: A problem statement that already signals constraint-awareness — not just user-need.

lessons.title: Constraint Mapping
lessons.goal: Explicitly define and evaluate every constraint in the challenge
lessons.what: Students create a constraint matrix — listing every constraint, categorising it, and assessing its impact on design decisions.
lessons.activityDetail: For each constraint: name it, categorise it (material / time / cost / user), rate its impact (high / medium / low), and identify what it rules out and what it still allows.
lessons.outcome: Students have a complete map of their design space — they know exactly what they can and cannot do.

lessons.title: Design Specification
lessons.goal: Define measurable success criteria that reflect constraint requirements
lessons.what: Students write success criteria that go beyond function — including criteria that specifically address the constraints they are working within.
lessons.activityDetail: Write 5–7 success criteria. At least 2 must directly address a constraint (e.g. 'uses only the provided materials', 'buildable in under 20 minutes'). Include 2–3 constraints as explicit parameters.
lessons.outcome: A specification that will hold the design accountable to its constraints — not just its function.

weeks.title: Ideation & Decision Making
weeks.desc: Students generate ideas specifically within their constraints — not despite them. They develop the most promising concept, analyse trade-offs honestly, and justify their final design decision.
weeks.focus: Moving from constrained ideas → developed concept → trade-off analysis → justified decision

lessons.title: Idea Generation Under Constraints
lessons.goal: Generate ideas that work within limits — not around them
lessons.what: Students brainstorm solutions with their constraint matrix visible at all times. Every idea must acknowledge its constraints explicitly.
lessons.activityDetail: Generate at least 6 ideas. For each: sketch the idea and annotate how it addresses the main constraints. Ideas that ignore constraints are flagged — not discarded, but reconsidered.
lessons.outcome: A set of ideas that are genuinely constraint-aware — not wishful thinking.

lessons.title: Concept Development
lessons.goal: Develop the most promising idea into a detailed, constraint-aware concept
lessons.what: Students select one idea and develop it fully — detailing materials, construction, use, and how each constraint is addressed.
lessons.activityDetail: Produce a detailed annotated sketch showing: how it functions, what materials are used and why, how each constraint is managed, and what trade-offs are being made.
lessons.outcome: A developed concept that proves the design can work within its constraints.

lessons.title: Trade-off Analysis
lessons.goal: Understand and articulate the compromises being made
lessons.what: Students analyse the trade-offs in their chosen design — what is gained, what is sacrificed, and whether the trade-off is justified.
lessons.activityDetail: For the chosen design: list what is gained (functionality, efficiency, accessibility) and what is lost (aesthetics, durability, flexibility). Justify why the gains outweigh the losses for this specific user and context.
lessons.outcome: Students practise honest design thinking — acknowledging imperfection while defending decisions.

lessons.title: Final Concept Selection
lessons.goal: Commit to and fully justify the chosen design direction
lessons.what: Students write a formal justification explaining why their chosen design is the best response to the challenge given its constraints.
lessons.activityDetail: Students write 200–300 words: why this solution, how it addresses each constraint, what trade-offs were made and why they are acceptable, and what would change with fewer constraints.
lessons.outcome: A clear, mature design rationale — the kind IB assessors look for in Criterion B.

weeks.title: Creation & Testing
weeks.desc: Students build efficiently within their constraints, test under realistic conditions, refine based on evidence, and evaluate how well the final design holds up against its specifications.
weeks.focus: Moving from concept → efficient prototype → tested solution → constraint evaluation

lessons.title: Prototype Creation
lessons.goal: Build a prototype that demonstrates the design working within its constraints
lessons.what: Students build a prototype using only the materials and time allowed. The constraint is part of the making — not suspended during it.
lessons.activityDetail: Build using only the specified materials. Time is tracked. Document decisions made during making — especially moments where the constraint forced a change of approach.
lessons.outcome: A prototype built within constraints — and a record of how constraints shaped the making process.

lessons.title: Testing Under Constraints
lessons.goal: Test the prototype under realistic conditions — not ideal ones
lessons.what: Students simulate the real-world conditions the design will face. Testing is not just 'does it work?' but 'does it work for this user in this context?'
lessons.activityDetail: Set up realistic test conditions based on context mapping. Tester role-plays as the target user. Designer observes and notes: what worked, what failed, where constraints created problems.
lessons.outcome: Evidence of how the design performs in its intended context — not just in ideal lab conditions.

lessons.title: Iteration
lessons.goal: Improve the solution while remaining within constraints
lessons.what: Students identify one meaningful improvement from testing and implement it — without violating their constraints. This forces prioritisation.
lessons.activityDetail: Students document: what feedback they received, what improvement they chose to make, why, and how they made it without exceeding the constraints. If a constraint had to flex, explain why.
lessons.outcome: A refined solution — and evidence of disciplined, constraint-aware iteration.

lessons.title: Evaluation Against Constraints
lessons.goal: Evaluate the final design specifically against its constraints
lessons.what: Students compare the final design to their success criteria — with special focus on how well the design managed its constraints.
lessons.activityDetail: For each success criterion: did the design meet it? What evidence supports this? For each constraint: was it respected? If not, why not? What would change with more time or different constraints?
lessons.outcome: A mature evaluation that addresses constraint management — not just product quality.

lessons.title: Final Presentation
lessons.goal: Present the design journey with emphasis on constraint management and decision-making
lessons.what: Students present their full process — with specific focus on how constraints shaped every decision, what trade-offs were made, and what they would do differently.
lessons.activityDetail: 5–7 minute presentation covering: the challenge and its constraints, key decisions made because of constraints, the prototype, testing findings, and evaluation. Class asks one question per presenter.
lessons.outcome: Students can articulate design thinking under pressure — a real-world professional skill.

assessment.focus: Constrained ideation, trade-off analysis, and justified selection
Efficient prototype built within stated constraints
Evaluation against constraints and success criteria

successCriteria: Identify and categorise all constraints in the design challenge
Research context and user needs that inform design decisions
Generate ideas that explicitly work within constraints — not around them
Analyse trade-offs honestly and justify the chosen design direction
Build a prototype using only specified materials and within time limits
Test under realistic conditions and document honest findings
Evaluate the final design against both functional criteria and constraint requirements
differentiation: Support: provide pre-structured constraint matrix, context map template, and justification sentence starters for students who need scaffolding
Advanced: assign tighter constraints, require trade-off analysis across three competing criteria, and challenge students to propose how constraints could be changed to improve the outcome
Language support: allow annotated visual presentations or recorded explanations as alternatives to written evaluation
units.realWorldConnection: Real-world design is never ideal. Every professional designer works within budgets, material limits, regulatory requirements, and time pressures. This unit brings that reality into the classroom.
units.progressionNote: This unit builds on MYP 3 systems thinking by adding the dimension of constraint management. Students must now not only consider multiple stakeholders but also work within explicit limits — moving closer to the conditions of the MYP 5 Personal Design Project.

units.title: The Urban Nexus
units.desc: Designing modular public furniture that must adhere to strict spatial, safety, and durability regulations.

units.title: The Personal Design Project
units.desc: The capstone unit where students define their own problem and execute the full design cycle independently.

units.title: Reflective Mastery
units.desc: A deep dive into impact assessment, evaluating the social and environmental consequences of design solutions.

demos.intro: Structured, inquiry-driven lessons designed to translate design thinking into meaningful classroom experiences. Each lesson is built to balance structure with creative freedom — keeping students engaged while building the critical thinking skills that matter beyond the classroom.

lessonStructure.title: Warm-up
lessonStructure.desc: Engage students with a hook or provocative question to spark curiosity.

lessonStructure.title: Inquiry
lessonStructure.desc: Problem exploration through research and collaborative brainstorming.

lessonStructure.title: Activity
lessonStructure.desc: Hands-on design work, prototyping, and active experimentation.

lessonStructure.title: Reflection
lessonStructure.desc: Group discussion, evaluation of work, and connecting back to learning goals.

lessons.title: Accessibility Audit Sprint
lessons.objective: Students analyse their environment to identify usability and accessibility challenges in common objects.

steps.desc: Students walk through the school space and document accessibility barriers they observe — physical, sensory, or cognitive.
Each student selects one barrier and frames it as a clear design problem statement.
Students rapidly sketch 3 possible improvements, focusing on function over aesthetics.
Class shares findings — groups discuss whose observation had the most human impact and why.

lessons.title: Empathy Interview Lab
lessons.objective: Students conduct structured interviews to understand user needs and perspectives deeply.

steps.desc: Students receive a brief: identify a daily frustration faced by someone at home — a parent, sibling, or grandparent.
Students prepare 5 open-ended interview questions — not 'do you like X?' but 'tell me about a time when...'
In pairs, students role-play the interview. One student is the 'user', one is the 'designer'. Then they switch.
Students write a user need statement: '[Person] needs a way to [goal] because [insight].' Shared with class.

lessons.title: Rapid Prototyping Sprint
lessons.objective: Students convert ideas into low-fidelity prototypes for quick testing and user feedback loops.

steps.desc: Students receive a pre-defined design brief (e.g. redesign a school stationery item for a student with limited hand mobility).
10 minutes: students sketch 3 distinct concepts — no judgment, quantity over quality at this stage.
Students build a rough physical prototype using only paper, tape, and scissors. Focus on communicating function, not finish.
Prototypes are tested by a peer acting as the target user. Designer observes silently, takes notes, then iterates.

lessons.title: UX Critique Circle
lessons.objective: Students evaluate peer designs using structured feedback frameworks based on design principles.

steps.desc: Each student posts their work-in-progress on the wall or desk with a brief description of the problem they were solving.
Students rotate and leave sticky-note feedback using a structured format: 'I notice...', 'I wonder...', 'What if...'
Designers read their feedback silently, identify one insight they hadn't considered, and note it in their process journal.
Class debrief: what did good feedback look like? What made some feedback more useful than others?

lessons.title: Design Constraint Challenge
lessons.objective: Students solve a specific problem within strict material or time limitations to foster innovative thinking.

steps.desc: Students receive the constraint: design a container that holds 10 marbles and can be dropped from 1 metre without breaking — using only one sheet of newspaper and 30cm of tape.
5 minutes planning only — no building yet. Students sketch their approach and identify the biggest risk.
20 minutes to build. No additional materials allowed. Teacher circulates asking 'why did you decide that?'
Drop test. Students whose design fails reflect: what would you change? Students whose design works reflect: what was the critical decision?
